summary: Cantrill argues that oversubscribed practitioner conferences like KubeCon should adopt double-blind review processes to ensure talks are selected on merit rather than the presenter's reputation or employer. Drawing from his experience organizing Systems We Love, he proposes a hybrid model: double-blind review until submissions are reduced to high-quality candidates, then de-blinding to shape the final program and amplify underrepresented voices. This approach led to surprising results at Systems We Love, where first-time speakers were accepted while 'big names' with weak submissions were rejected.
key_points: Double-blind review ensures content quality is based on submission merit, not presenter reputation or employer
A hybrid approach works well: blind review to filter quality, then de-blind to shape final program
First-time speakers often have excellent submissions that would be overlooked without blind review
Even 'big names' submit weak proposals - not every swing is a home run
13% acceptance rate means many quality submissions will be rejected regardless of process
money_quotes: Any oversubscribed conference needs to be double-blind to a very significant degree.
De-blinding at this point assures that the quality of the content is primarily due to the merit of the submission – not due to the particulars of the submitter.
Several 'big names' had submissions that we rejected while blinded – but looking at their submissions, the submissions themselves just weren't that great!
At least a quarter of the speakers were presenting for the first time.
I have been troubled by the strangulation of practitioner conferences by academic computer science.
key_insight: Double-blind review is essential for oversubscribed conferences to ensure merit-based selection, but a hybrid approach that de-blinds for final program curation can help surface underrepresented voices while maintaining quality standards.
